message:
fix(install.sh): capture service status output unconditionally in readiness-check failure path

The `ensure_remote_http_client_readiness` helper lands in its err
branch when the installed background service is running but the MCP
HTTP endpoint is still not healthy — exactly the case where the
operator needs to see the `service status` output to diagnose.

Previously:

  if service_output=$("$DEST/$BIN_CLI" service status 2>&1); then
      ... print each line ...
  fi

That only ran the print loop when `service status` exited 0. But
`service status` exits non-zero for most of the failing service
states (broken config, unit activating, socket bound but not
accepting, etc.) — precisely the ones the operator is trying to
debug. So the diagnostic was silently suppressed exactly when it
was most needed.

Fix: capture output unconditionally with `|| true`, then guard the
print loop on the captured output being non-empty. Exit code is
ignored — the `service status` output itself carries the relevant
state, and we already know we're in a failure branch because the
outer readiness probe just gave up.

Also:
- Declare `service_output` `local` (was implicitly global inside
  the function; harmless but inconsistent with the surrounding
  helpers).
- Collapse the duplicate `[ -n "$line" ] && ...` guard into a
  single `if [ -n "$line" ]; then ... fi` block so the two log
  calls run together under one guard.

files.patch:
@@ -2828,11 +2828,18 @@ ensure_remote_http_client_readiness() {
   # want to start the server manually), but echo the service status
   # verbatim so the failure mode is obvious.
   err "Background service was installed, but the MCP HTTP endpoint is still not healthy."
-  service_output=""
-  if service_output=$("$DEST/$BIN_CLI" service status 2>&1); then
+  # `service status` returns non-zero when the service is failing (which is
+  # exactly the case that lands us here), so capture its output
+  # unconditionally — otherwise a `if service_output=$(...); then` would drop
+  # the diagnostic lines on the floor precisely when they're most needed.
+  local service_output=""
+  service_output=$("$DEST/$BIN_CLI" service status 2>&1 || true)
+  if [ -n "$service_output" ]; then
     while IFS= read -r line; do
-      [ -n "$line" ] && err "  status: ${line}"
-      [ -n "$line" ] && verbose "remote_http_readiness:status ${line}"
+      if [ -n "$line" ]; then
+        err "  status: ${line}"
+        verbose "remote_http_readiness:status ${line}"
+      fi
     done <<< "$service_output"
   fi
   err "Diagnose with:  ${DEST}/${BIN_CLI} service status"
